justification: All pass criteria met: Breaking change clearly identified ('fundamental change to the core domain model'). New minimum-cardinality invariant explicitly stated (BR-1: at least one Recipient). Data migration impact for 2M records covered (BR-5, AC-8, edge case for dirty data). External API breakage identified with versioning strategy (BR-6, BR-7, AC-4). 'Delivery preferences' ambiguity surfaced as first escalation with options. Architectural concerns flagged via escalations (though MJ not named explicitly, the escalations clearly call for architectural/product decisions). Confidence level 55, well under the 75 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough domain analysis — 9 business rules with clear invariant classification and justification
Delivery preferences ambiguity surfaced prominently as first escalation with concrete options
Data migration impact comprehensively covered including dirty data edge case
External API breakage addressed with v1/v2 versioning strategy, webhook implications, and partner migration timeline escalation
Per-recipient delivery status tracking identified as invariant (BR-8), catching the notification logic redesign need
Confidence at 55 with well-articulated low-confidence areas and explicit assumptions
Raised a critical ambiguity not in the rubric: whether multi-recipient means multi-stop or multi-package — demonstrates deep domain thinking
Edge cases are practical and operationally grounded (partial cancellation, dirty migration data, abuse limits)
Did not explicitly name MJ for architectural migration plan — escalations are framed generically rather than delegating to a specific team agent
Previous invariant ('A Shipment has exactly one Recipient') not explicitly called out as removed/broken — the breaking nature is implied but the old invariant is not stated and then negated

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: Breaking change clearly identified ('This is not a minor schema change — it redefines what a Shipment is'). New minimum-cardinality invariant stated (BR-1: at least one recipient). Data migration impact for 2M existing records noted (BR-4, AC-2). External API breakage identified (BR-7, AC-4, escalations). 'Delivery preferences' ambiguity surfaced extensively across ubiquitous language, edge cases, and low-confidence areas. Architectural concerns flagged via escalations (though MJ not named explicitly, escalations to product team and architectural decisions are raised). Confidence level 45, well below the 75 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough analysis that goes well beyond minimum requirements — identifies additional ambiguities (billing model, recipient ordering, primary recipient, physical packaging model) that demonstrate deep domain reasoning
Business rules are well-structured with clear invariant justifications and testable assertions, far exceeding the expected depth
Edge cases are comprehensive and practical — partial cancellation, in-flight migration, privacy concerns, max recipients, billing attribution
Confidence level of 45 is appropriately low given the number of unresolved ambiguities, with clear articulation of high vs low confidence areas
Stakeholder impact analysis is thorough, covering 7 distinct groups with specific impacts
Escalations provide concrete options with recommendations, showing domain authority rather than just flagging problems
Does not explicitly name MJ as the escalation target for architectural migration planning — escalations are framed generically rather than routing to the team architect
Correctly identifies the Recipient entity promotion from value object to entity — a subtle but important DDD insight
Acceptance criteria are well-formed Given/When/Then covering both happy path and validation edge cases

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: Breaking change clearly identified ('This is not a simple schema migration — it redefines what a Shipment *is*'). New minimum-cardinality invariant stated (BR-1, BR-5). Data migration impact for 2M records noted (BR-6, AC-6, business_impact.risk). External API breakage identified (BR-7, AC-5, edge case on primary recipient). 'Delivery preferences' ambiguity surfaced as explicit escalation. Architectural concerns flagged via escalations (would route to MJ). Confidence level 52, well below 75 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough breaking change analysis — goes beyond identifying the break to articulating exactly why it breaks (aggregate root boundary, downstream processes, external integrations)
BR-1 through BR-8 provide a comprehensive invariant analysis, clearly distinguishing true invariants from policy rules with strong justifications
Delivery preferences ambiguity surfaced as a formal escalation with concrete options and a recommendation
Data migration impact explicitly addressed with rollback requirements and backward-compatibility constraints
External API breakage thoroughly covered — identified the need for a primary recipient selection rule and versioning strategy
Edge cases are excellent — billing model ambiguity, concurrent modification, migration rollback, and same-address recipients all identified
Confidence level of 52 is well-calibrated given the number of undefined business decisions
Stakeholder impact analysis is comprehensive, covering seven distinct groups with specific impacts
Acceptance criteria are concrete and testable, covering both happy path and backward-compatibility scenarios
Surfaced the 'Partially Delivered' composite status as a new operational state requiring support team training — a non-obvious operational impact
